title: A view to Roche Rock (upcoming)
subtitle: A narrative-driven, fully interactive map exploring prehistoric landscapes in Cornwall
body: In Summer 2025, I visited Cornwall to stay with my friend Jack (@achy.legs.jack), an archaeologist who focuses on geospatial data visualisation and analysis. Among the numerous prehistoric burial chambers, stone circles, and standing stones we visited is a site called Roche Rock, a distinctive stone outcrop that sits stark in the Cornish landscape. Atop Roche Rock are the remnants of a medieval chapel dedicated to St. Michael, with the chapel window perfectly framing the scenery behind it. In any landscape, Roche Rock would stand out. In the context of Cornish history and heritage, Roche Rock is not only iconic, but also poses an intriguing question about the relationship between people, culture, and the physical landscape. We wondered if a prominent, visually memorable geological feature - one that is located notably close to numerous Neolithic and Bronze Age sites - could have served as a spiritual symbol for the people who inhabited ancient Cornwall. A 2023 journal article argued that prehistoric sites in Bodmin Moor, just ~15 miles from Roche Rock, “pay tribute” to another unique geological feature, Rough Tor. Jack and I wanted to explore this theory using Roche Rock as our case study, combining our respective skill sets to create a fully interactive, narrative-driven multimedia storymap that allows visitors to explore the interconnected prehistoric landscape through maps, view sheds, 360° imagery, drone shots, and 3D models. We’re currently processing our data and building our website, and hope to launch the project in Summer 2026.

media.caption: Jack and I near Roche Rock
Jack flying the drone
Getting some drone orbits around Roche Rock
Helman Tor, another significant feature and prehistoric site near Roche Rock
The view to Roche Rock from Helman Tor
A 360° atop Roche Rock
Roche Rock from above, taken on a DJI Mini 4k

title: Fountains Abbey: Revealed!
subtitle: How can innovative digital experiences enable archaeologists to communicate complex geophysics to the public?
body: In 2025, Visualising Heritage partnered with the National Trust to create a standalone interactive digital experience to put visitors in the shoes of an archaeologist at the medieval site of Fountains Abbey in North Yorkshire. Every year, hundreds of thousands of visitors flock to Fountains Abbey to walk among the ruins of what was once the largest monastery in England. Above the ground, the structures that remain are exceptional. However, beneath the seemingly untouched East Green, lies a trove of archaeology that is neither seen nor heard in the broader narrative of Fountains Abbey, yet fundamentally changes the story of the site. I was tasked with designing the entire experience, from brainstorming how we intuitively showcase complex geophysics, to structuring the narrative, to building the interactive tour. First, our team conducted high-resolution geophysical surveys using Ground Penetrating Radar (GPR) and Magnetometry on the East Green, confirming previously discovered features and uncovering new ones. The geophysical data highlighted fascinating features that visitors can’t engage with on-site, such as a large cemetery buried under the East Green, a mysterious row of burials away from the graveyard, and the foundations of a massive industrial complex. Our goal was to tell the story of Fountains Abbey through these findings, bridging the gap between high-level geophysical data and information meant for broad audiences of all ages. We settled on building an on-site, “AR-lite” experience that simulated the feeling of Augmented Reality without putting excess strain on everyday consumer devices. Using a pro-grade 360° camera, we captured high resolution panoramas along various spots along the East Green where geophysical survey had been conducted. Those photos were imported into Blender, where we created a projection that enabled us to place the geophysical data onto the East Green, viewable from a first-person POV. I then created animations in Adobe After Effects using the geophysics data to highlight archaeological features that appeared in the imagery, allowing users to interpret the data the same way an archaeologist would. All media, including animations, videos, photos, and the reprojected geophysics were imported into 3DVista, a platform used primarily for virtual real estate tours. We created an annotated map of Fountains Abbey with Points of Interest marked along the East Green, with each location consisting of a 360° base panorama layer (taken from the same spot in real life) and interactive buttons to cue text, animations, or video. We formed our narrative approach around providing visitors agency and allowing them to decide where to go, in which order, and what they were interested in learning. This non-linear approach gave us additional flexibility with the narrative approach, with each location uncovering a piece of the puzzle to understanding the broader story of Fountains Abbey. The experience is filled with various types of media spanning different genres, from raw geophysical data to video clips and pop culture references. Fountains Abbey: Revealed! was evaluated with members of the public during the CBA Festival of Archaeology 2025 at Fountains Abbey. Our evaluation results demonstrated the impact of using creative visualisations and storytelling in communicating archaeological and geophysical data, especially to a broad audience. If you’d like to try it out for yourself, we’ve created an at-home version here (works on all devices but best on a tablet): Fountains Abbey Revealed!

media.caption: A screenshot of the experience
360° photo capture at Fountains Abbey using an Insta360 Titan
Results from the Ground Penetrating Radar overlaid onto the East Green
Testing the experience on-site
Setting up the geophysical survey equipment
Talking to visitors about our project during the Festival of Archaeology 2025

title: Storytelling through 3D Printing and Projection Mapping
subtitle: How can the techniques archaeologists use help with public consultations and placemaking?
introText: Designed the narrative approach and produced animated stories on heritage and placemaking for a Projection Augmented Relief Model communication tool.
body:
As a researcher for Visualising Heritage at the University of Bradford, I was involved in a project with the Bradford City Council to tell the story of an area of the city through a cutting-edge visualisation technique called Projection Augmented Relief Mapping (PARM). I crafted bite-sized animations about the history and infrastructure of Bradford that were then projected onto a 3D printed model of the city, bringing the plastic to life. Our evaluations demonstrated our model’s huge potential in education, policymaking, and civic engagement.

At Visualising Heritage, I largely focused on researching ways to effectively convey complex archaeology, with an emphasis on creatively visualising geospatial data. Additionally, as a team of archaeologists, our work centred heavily on establishing and communicating context. For this project, we decided to utilise innovative, rapid capture technologies used on archaeological sites combined with our experience distilling geospatial information to broad audiences to create an interactive, audience-led 3D printed model that brought the story of Southern Gateway to life.

During the capture phase of the project, the Visualising Heritage team at the University of Bradford automated a drone to systematically fly over and photograph a section of Bradford city called Southern Gateway. These photos were stitched together to create a high resolution orthographic aerial image as well as a detailed 3D model using Structure from Motion (SfM) photogrammetry. The model was scaled down, split into tiles, and 3D printed using a white matte bioplastic filament to create a 1.8m x 1.2m sized model.

We took a multipronged approach for the narrative structure and content types. The primary brief was to generate conversation and understanding around regenerating the Southern Gateway as part of a significant government infrastructure investment, particularly focusing on the potential upheaval of the Bradford Interchange railway station.

Our first goal was to create a series of short stories that provided audiences with the necessary context behind Southern Gateway, such as its topography, history of public transport, and publicly-accessible green spaces. We recorded voiceovers and created animations for eight stories (about two minutes long each) that audiences could select from. We also created numerous static data layers (e.g. land use, historic maps, gradients) that provided additional context and information that could inform peoples’ decisions about regeneration. Finally, we created a series of “drop-in” points that displayed 360° images taken from the ground at various places in Southern Gateway.

This project was a Knowledge Transfer Partnership with the City of Bradford Metropolitan District Council to develop novel methodologies to digitally record an area of Bradford city, 3D print a scaled down version of it, and project curated content onto the model to communicate the complexities involving city regeneration. This technology, called a Projection Augmented Relief Model (PARM), was designed as part of a larger toolkit to be used in public consultations regarding placemaking.

To set it all up, a short throw projector was mounted above the model and a TV was placed behind the setup to provide additional information and imagery. All the content and computing was run via a PC using MadMapper, a projection mapping software that allowed us to import the 3D digital model to accurately project maps and animations onto the physical model, as well as create cues and scenes to display content on both the printed model and the contextual TV. A touchscreen was mounted beside the model to allow users to trigger content.

This tool was evaluated with members of the public in December 2024 and January 2025, with anonymised survey responses demonstrating significant potential for PARM as an effective communication tool in planning, construction, and education.

media.caption: Satellite imagery projected onto the 3d printed model
Focus group members using our PARM
Top-down view of the model and back screen

summary: Our team developed a unique communication tool using 3d printing and projectors to bring a city to life.

title: Bringing a medieval home to life through rapid 3D capture
subtitle: Using experimental tools and techniques to rapidly document and visualise a heritage property
coverAlt: "X-ray" view of the ground floor of the property
body: In 2025, I put in a successful bid on behalf of Visualising Heritage that won the team a free stay at “The White House”, a 13th century homestead in Shropshire, UK. We used this opportunity to record, document, and interpret the heritage of the site, test out new equipment, upskill, and take some time away from campus. We documented the property internally and externally through various rapid capture techniques using drones, 360° cameras, handheld laser scanners, terrestrial laser scanners, mirrorless cameras, and spatial microphones. Using a combination of these methods allowed us to create different types of digital representations, from high-resolution 3D photogrammetry models to interactive 360° tours of the property. Experimenting and training up on these novel capture techniques was invaluable, as it gave each team member the opportunity to test new approaches and determine their applicability in our current workflows. This opportunity also provided us a testing bed for experimental storytelling and interpretation using digital surrogates. Outputs from our stay at The White House are still being produced, and any media will be added below. For more information about the White House, visit the Landmark Trust website.

title: Threads Through Time - an immersive film
subtitle: Exploring the heritage of Yorkshire and the history of its textile industry through immersive film.
coverAlt: The "Tower of Wool" sculpture atop Penistone Hill
introText: Filmed and produced 360° content for use in immersive projection cylinders, virtual tours, VR headsets, and social media.
body:
“Threads Through Time” is an immersive film slated as part of Bradford 2025’s City of Culture programme, led by the Visualising Heritage team at the University of Bradford. I worked as the director and editor on the piece, using pro-grade 360° cameras, 360° action cameras, VR microphones, laser scanners, and drones to film and stitch together a fully immersive film meant for viewing in the university’s 360° projection cylinder.

We worked with local residents to showcase their art, hobbies, specialisations, and daily lives, weaving together their stories with the complicated, intertwined history of Bradford and Yorkshire. We filmed across the county with an Insta360 Titan, from a soon-to-be-demolished car park in city centre to the top of Penistone Hill on an incredibly windy, wet day. Featuring historic sites such as the Twelve Apostles stone circle and the Hirst Wood ancient woodland helped merge the themes of modernity with local heritage.

I edited the 8k equirectangular footage in Adobe Premiere Pro, using After Effects to rotoscope subjects and create animations. Music was recorded in the field, with additional audio recorded by participants and obtained through an audio library. I also created my own set of automations in Photoshop, allowing me to quickly edit out tripod legs from the 360° footage. Lastly, I incorporated different types of media, from archival 2D media to renders from laser scans taken by our team.

The full piece will be shown at the University of Bradford and will be catalogued and archived as part of the broader BD25 City of Culture legacy.

media.caption: 12 Apostles, Yorkshire, in 360°

title: History in Macro - Artifact Photography
subtitle: High resolution, detailed studio photos of historical artifacts taken from up close
body: The archaeology department at the University of Bradford has uncovered some fantastic artifacts, from Neolithic arrowheads to medieval ceramics. I wanted to take high resolution, close-up photos of these objects to document them, practice my macro + studio photography skills, and use the images/video as publicity material for the department. I used a Sony a7r IV with a variety of macro lenses, including a Sigma 105mm, a Laowa 25mm Ultra Macro, and a Laowa 24mm 2x Macro Probe lens. Objects were shot in our makeshift photo studio using widely-available lights, tripods, and turntables. To achieve maximum detail, I used a technique called “focus stacking”, where multiple images consisting of different focal points are blended together in post-processing, creating a final output image that has the entirety of the object in perfect focus. In addition to photos, I took videos of some of the objects as well, with my favourite being this video of a glass shampoo bottle excavated in Lister Park, Bradford.

media.caption: A neolithic barbed arrowhead
An amber bead
A faceted bead
A flint arrowhead in black and white
An early 20th century coin

title: Friday Nights are Amami Nights!
subtitle: Making a luxury perfume advertisement for an excavated artifact
coverCaption: Amami WaveSet - excavated Summer 2025 in Lister Park, Bradford
coverAlt: Amami WaveSet - excavated Summer 2025 in Lister Park, Bradford
body:
In Summer 2025, the University of Bradford’s department of archaeology ran summer excavations for the public in Lister Park. One of the units managed to find a 20th century corked glass bottle containing an unidentified liquid, branded “Amami”.

A bit of light research revealed that Amami was a British brand specialising in hair products, including lotions and shampoos, inspired by the Japanese pine.

Along with macro photos of the artifact, I wanted to do something more creative. I ended up filming b-roll of the bottle in Visualising Heritage’s studio, using fun lights, lenses, and compositions to create a video of the bottle in the style of a luxury perfume advertisement. Artifacts are inherently fascinating - why can’t we make them look cool as well?

media.caption: The front of the bottle
The back of the bottle
Amami WaveSet - Found 2025, Lister Park

title: Lost Treasures of Egypt
subtitle: Follow archaeologists as they uncover ancient tombs, temples, and treasures across Egypt.
introText: Conducted production research and fact-checking for two National Geographic archaeology docuseries, coordinating with archaeological teams across the Middle East.
body: Lost Treasures of Egypt is a National Geographic/Windfall Films docuseries following international teams of archaeologists as they excavate some of the most incredible sites in Egypt, from massive necropolises to hidden tombs. I joined Windfall Films as a production researcher, leaning heavily on my background in archaeology to dig into the detail behind the stories we were telling. That meant extensive desk-based research into ancient Egyptian history, mythology, funerary practices, and the archaeological evidence underpinning each episode's narrative. Alongside that, I conducted archival research, trawling through field notes, sketches, and reports from 19th and 20th century Egyptologists. A significant part of my role was centred around people and logistics. I coordinated with field teams who were planning their annual excavation missions in Egypt, helping align their schedules and research priorities with the production's needs. I also communicated regularly with archaeologists and experts, who provided the scientific rigour behind each episode. Additionally, I worked extensively with museums and collections around the world, both for research purposes and to license media. I also oversaw voiceover recording with talent to ensure pronunciations and information were authentic and accurate. Lastly, I fact-checked each episode in coordination with National Geographic’s editorial team in the US, ensuring our show met the rigorous standards of Nat Geo. To see where you can watch the show, visit Disney+ or National Geographic.

links.label: National Geographic — Lost Treasures of Egypt

title: Lost Treasures of the Bible - National Geographic
subtitle: The stories from The Bible, interpreted and challenged through active archaeology in the Middle East.
coverCaption: A still frame from the first season of the show
coverAlt: A still frame from the first season of the show
body: Lost Treasures of the Bible is a National Geographic/Windfall Films docuseries following archaeological teams across the Middle East as they excavate the cities and civilisations behind the Bible's most famous stories. My research role on this series covered a wide range of disciplines, including biblical archaeology, ancient Near Eastern history and mythology, and the broader religious and cultural contexts that connect these sites to the stories people know. I relied on my background in archaeology to dig into the detail behind the stories we were telling, providing scientific rigour and deep archival research. As with Lost Treasures of Egypt, I worked closely with archaeologists and field teams, coordinated with museums and collections for research and media licensing, and fact-checked each episode against National Geographic's editorial standards. Organising shoots across Egypt, Jordan, and Türkiye was complex enough, but several episodes required us to work in active conflict zones, including Israel and Iraq. Getting crews, archaeologists, and equipment into those regions safely demanded constant communication, careful planning, and the agility to quickly change plans if necessary. To find out more, visit Disney+ or National Geographic.

title: An Avebury Story - a 360° interactive story
subtitle: Creating an interactive VR game set in the Neolithic stone circles at Avebury
coverAlt: Splash screen for the VR experience
introText: Practice-based MSc dissertation exploring the efficacy of VR, 360° film, and participant agency in communicating archaeology. Ranked 1st in the programme.
body: An Avebury Story is an immersive, choose-your-own-adventure VR experience I created for my Master’s dissertation in Digital Archaeology. Transport yourself to the Neolithic stone circles at Avebury, Wiltshire, where you get to choose which path to traverse and what stories to experience. Filmed on consumer-grade 360° cameras and built in Unity, An Avebury Story is optimised for Meta Quest headsets and is also playable in YouTube’s 360° mode. The project was a form of practice-based research, where the creative process itself was the data. By designing, building, and testing the experience, I was able to assess the real affordances and limitations of using immersive media to communicate archaeology, both in theory and in practice. The experience was shot entirely on location at Avebury using a consumer-grade 360° camera. I wrote and filmed six stories, each set at a different location within the site, covering topics ranging from a medieval murder mystery to the spiritual significance of the stones. I also featured voices from the local community and volunteers, offering perspectives outside traditional archaeology. The editing and post-production were done in Adobe Premiere Pro and After Effects, with the interactive VR environment built in Unity using a plugin called Cutting Room, developed by Digital Creativity Labs at the University of York. Users experienced the story through a Meta Quest 2 headset, determining which path through Avebury they wanted to take. The experience was tested with 100 participants (both in-person through VR headsets and remotely through a YouTube 360° version), followed by a detailed survey measuring immersion, presence, emotion, and engagement. The results were overwhelmingly positive, with nearly everyone saying they’d learned something and that they'd want to see a similar project at other heritage sites. Beyond the quantitative data, what the project revealed was something more fundamental: that narrative will always outperform technology. Once the novelty of VR wore off, participants were more engaged with the stories rather than the novel tech. The stories that resonated most were the ones where the format served the content, where the 360° environment added a dimension to the story that traditional film can’t recreate. Building An Avebury Story also surfaced real challenges around community collaboration, ownership, and representation. One participant withdrew their recorded piece after testing, raising important questions about who controls heritage narratives and how digital archaeologists navigate that responsibly. The project demonstrated that immersive, interactive heritage storytelling is not only achievable on a modest budget, but capable of forging genuine connections between audiences and archaeology. As the tools become more accessible and the technology matures, the potential for experiences like these, built by archaeologists and for everyone, is enormous.

title: How to find buried treasure in York
subtitle: A lighthearted exploration of how York’s heritage and archaeology live in the present day.
introText: Filmed and edited dozens of videos for the Archaeology Department's social media channels, including faculty interviews and mini-documentaries.
body: This is a short film I made during my Master’s program in Digital Archaeology at the University of York. It’s a lighthearted investigation into what defines “treasure” archaeologically and ties into the history and culture of York. I filmed everything on a Sony a7iii and edited the final piece in DaVinci Resolve.

title: Prehistoric Firelighting - Experimental Archaeology
subtitle: A short video documenting the prehistoric firestarting process.
coverCaption: The full video with Chris Woodland
coverAlt: The full video with Chris Woodland
body:
As a publicity assistant at the University of York, I produced this film documenting experimental archaeology in action. I love experimental archaeology, and believe it to be one of the most effective ways of not just communicating heritage, but also empathising with the people who came before us by trialling and testing their techniques. This video, featuring experimental archaeologist Chris Woodland, explores a prehistoric firestarting process that many neolithic and prehistoric peoples would have employed in the UK and mainland Europe.

I pitched, filmed, and edited the piece, and shot it on-location at the University of York’s YEAR Centre, dedicated specifically to experimental archaeology.

The film was published through the department's social channels as part of the ongoing content program I managed during my time there.

title: A look inside an Ancient DNA lab
subtitle: What does a bioarchaeologist and ancient geneticist do, actually?
coverCaption: The video with Dr. Nathan Wales
coverAlt: The video with Dr. Nathan Wales
body:
Working as a publicity assistant for the University of York's Department of Archaeology, I produced this short film about the university's ancient DNA laboratory, one of the specialist research facilities within the department, along with an interview with Dr. Nathan Wales, a bioarchaeologist and ancient geneticist in the department. The piece was designed to give a general audience an accessible window into how ancient DNA analysis works and why it matters for understanding the past.

I also took 360° images, video, and timelapses inside the lab, which were then passed on to the university's IT department to build an interactive walkthrough of the aDNA lab.

I handled the full production from research, scripting, filming on-site in the lab, to editing. The video was published across the department's social media channels as part of a broader program of research communication content I was producing throughout my time at York.

Along with a stand-up interview with Dr. Nathan Wales, filming the b-roll involved decontaminating myself and my equipment in order to record in the highly sensitive aDNA laboratory!

media.caption: Dr. Nathan Wales
Inside the lab
Inside the fridge

title: The Huntingdon Exhibit - A VR Museum
subtitle: A fully interactive virtual museum looking at artifacts, 3D models, media, and more from the Star Carr Mesolithic site.
coverAlt: A screenshot of the virtual museum
body: The Huntingdon Exhibit is a virtual reality museum dedicated to the excavations undertaken at the Mesolithic site of Star Carr, Yorkshire. The main room was modelled after the Huntingdon Room at King’s Manor in York, and the displays incorporate photos, video, text, audio, 3D objects, and interactive elements. The exhibit was mostly built in the now-defunct Mozilla Spoke, with some 3D modelling done in Blender. The final project was an interactive, multiplayer, multimedia digital museum that allowed users to pick up, throw, wear, and interact with the virtual artifacts.

Top view of the exhibit, including lighting and windows
What the Mozilla Spoke builder looked like

title: Go There - CNN
subtitle: A daily mini-doc series exploring a variety of human-centred stories, from climate change to social justice.
introText: Produced 50+ mini-documentaries with a combined 60+ million views for CNN Digital's daily show covering climate change, indigenous rights, and global affairs.
body:
I was a video producer on “Go There”, CNN’s daily digital news show that took viewers to a new part of the world every day, covering stories that primetime news often looked past. Our small team based in NYC published hundreds of mini-documentaries, filmed in more than 55 countries, and became one of the most watched social media news programs in the world.

As a video producer, I handled every stage of production for my pieces from pitching and research through to filming, scripting, fact-checking, and publishing. Our videos were human-centred, character-driven stories that explored a single topic, spanning climate change, social justice, international affairs, conflict, and more. I field produced multiple videos, shooting B-cam and conducting the interviews, and also worked with CNN’s global pool of correspondents and contributors to source stories from all corners of the globe.

Go There ended its run having published hundreds of mini-documentaries, with my videos alone accruing a combined reach of more than 60 million views on Facebook Watch. Go There was nominated for a Shorty Award in 2020, after one of our videos that gave an inside look into the start of the Covid-19 pandemic became the most-watched news video on Facebook of all time.

media.caption: My CNN reel from 2021
The struggle for Native Americans to get water
CNN Go There promo image

title: Mini Doc: following NYC’s yellow cab drivers during the height of the pandemic
subtitle: A mini-documentary I produced that follows yellow cab drivers during the height of the pandemic in NYC.
body: This was the first piece I field produced for CNN Digital after Covid-19 had taken hold, and for New York's yellow cab industry, the pandemic was only another item to add to a list of rapidly growing concerns. Medallion debt, the rise of Uber and Lyft, and plummeting ridership had put immense pressure on yellow cab drivers for years. Drivers who had staked their livelihoods on medallions worth over a million dollars at their peak were now sitting on assets worth a fraction of that. The pandemic was the final blow. This story follows three drivers whose lives were intertwined through personal loss, and the subsequent solidarity formed between them, looking through the eyes of their families, frontline activists, and industry experts. Producing this during Covid meant working under strict health protocols and keeping the crew as small as possible. Every interview required careful coordination around medical guidelines, background checks, and safety protocols. Filming inside a moving taxi with a driver added another layer of complexity, both logistically and in terms of managing risk for our subjects. I conducted the research, interviews, b-cam shooting, scripting, fact-checking, and final publication, and wrote an accompanying article for CNN. Click here to watch the video and read the article.

title: The struggle for Native Americans to get water in South Dakota
subtitle: A story of how people from a neglected community are helping to provide their fellow tribe members access to basic resources.
body:
This is one of my favourite stories I worked on at CNN. I produced and filmed (B-cam) this piece.

For the Oglala Lakota people on Pine Ridge Indian Reservation in South Dakota, many don’t have access to running water. The reservation is plagued with unemployment, low life expectancy and poverty, but one organization is hoping to alleviate the conditions by connecting homes to piped water for free.

In 2019, I reached out to Running Strong, a nonprofit based out of Pine Ridge who were working towards improving the general infrastructure and livelihoods of the people living on the reservation. We followed Ken Lone Elk, Running Strong’s project coordinator, and his partners as they provided support to various residents.

I pitched the story, found the characters, filmed B-cam, interviewed the subjects, scripted the story, and handled the final fact-check and publishing of the video. The story was a success on Facebook Watch, with more than 4 million people watching the piece.

media.caption: Residents on Pine Ridge sometimes have to collect water from a frozen creek
Mark Cottier, Pine Ridge resident with no access to piped water

Running Strong contractors connecting a home to piped water
A pile of dishes in a sink with no water

title: BEME News
subtitle: A weekly desk explainer show breaking down complex topics, from human psychology to international affairs.
introText: Produced 100+ episodes of a weekly desk explainer show for CNN's BEME News YouTube channel.
body: I worked as researcher and associate producer for BEME News, an independent digital media company that was absorbed by CNN Digital. I was part of a small team producing weekly desk explainer videos covering a breadth of topics, providing deep-dives into subjects like global affairs, climate change, infrastructure, and human psychology. At BEME, I pitched stories, conducted research, found industry experts to interview, collected and licensed stock images and archival media, and did the final fact-checking, QC’ing, subtitling, and publishing for the episodes. Along with the desk explainer show, we also published longer field pieces where a producer would cover a story on location. For these, I helped with the research and background, editing, and fact-checking, QC, and publication. Each episode was between 10-15 minutes long and published on YouTube under the BEME News YouTube channel. Visit the channel to see the entire catalog.
